scripts.title: Why Your Meta Ads Aren't Getting Leads
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting at a desk or casual setup, direct eye contact with camera. Natural lighting. Relaxed posture. Let him gesture naturally when explaining each mistake — feel conversational, not scripted.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service business owners are making one of these three mistakes. check which one is killing your leads

captions.label: B
captions.text: your ad might be fine. but if you're running it to the wrong audience or sending them somewhere confusing, you won't get leads. here are the three biggest killers

captions.label: C
captions.text: bad targeting, bad landing page, bad follow-up. pick one and fix it

lines.text: So you're running Meta ads, spending twenty-five bucks a day, and getting nothing. Let me tell you the three mistakes that are probably killing your leads right now.

First one — you're targeting too broad. You're saying yes to every homeowner in a fifty-mile radius. But you only service a specific area, right? Tighten that radius. Target people who are actually close enough to call you. Waste of money otherwise.

Second — your landing page is confusing. They click your ad and end up somewhere that doesn't match what the ad promised. Or there's friction. Too many steps. No clear way to book or call. People bounce. Make it dead simple — one goal, one button, clear next step.

Third — you're not following up. Someone fills out a form or calls and you ghost them. Or you take six hours to respond. These businesses move fast. Respond in minutes, not hours. That's the difference between a lead and a lost opportunity.
Fix any one of these and you'll see a shift. It's usually not the ad — it's where it's going or what happens after.

scripts.title: The offer that makes people actually call
scripts.film: Screen record yourself in Meta Ads Manager, navigating to an ad creative or draft. Show the ad copy section clearly. Point to different parts of the offer as you talk through them. Keep it relaxed — this is a walkthrough, not a presentation.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your offer needs to answer one question: why would i pick up the phone right now instead of next week?

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners write offers that are too generic. show the specific result, the timeline, and remove the friction to call. that's the difference between okay and calls.

captions.label: C
captions.text: people don't call because your service is good. they call because your offer makes it easy.

lines.text: Most offers sound the same. Here's what actually makes someone pick up the phone.

First thing — stop saying 'call for a free estimate.' Everyone says that. Instead, tell them what they're getting and when. Like 'Free inspection today, same-day quote.' That's specific. That's a real reason to call now.

Second thing — address the friction. People delay because they're thinking 'this will take forever' or 'it'll be expensive.' Your offer should answer that. Something like 'Most jobs scheduled within 48 hours' or 'Fixed pricing, no surprises.' That removes the doubt.
And third — make the call easy. Not just 'call now.' Give them one number, one way to reach you. Remove the options. Simplicity wins.
An offer that works isn't about being clever. It's about being clear and honest about what they get and why today matters.

scripts.title: What $25/day actually gets you
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting or standing casually, well-lit face, direct eye contact. Relaxed energy — like he's explaining this to a friend over coffee. No graphics needed; his confidence in the numbers is the hook.

captions.label: A
captions.text: $25/day isn't tiny. it's actually enough to get real results if you know how to set it up.

captions.label: B
captions.text: $25/day = roughly 750 impressions + 15-25 clicks + 2-4 qualified leads per month. depends on your area and offer, but that's the ballpark for most service businesses.

captions.label: C
captions.text: small budgets work. you just need the right structure.

lines.text: so everyone asks me — what does $25 a day actually get you? and the answer is way more than people think.
on $25 a day, you're looking at around 750 impressions per month. that's people seeing your ad. from that, you get maybe 15 to 25 clicks depending on how tight your targeting is.

and here's the real number — 2 to 4 qualified leads per month. could be more if you're a plumber in a busy area, could be less if you're competing in a saturated market. but that's the real-world range.
the key is you're not throwing it at a wide audience. you're being surgical with location, service type, and audience fit. that's why the leads actually matter.
people think small budgets can't work. but if you set it up right, $25 a day is legit.

scripts.title: Referrals are keeping you stuck
scripts.film: Film yourself at your desk or standing casually. Speak directly to camera. Each slide should show the bold text clearly on dark background — let the words do the work while you deliver the message naturally.

captions.label: A
captions.text: referrals feel safe but they're capping your growth. here's why.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your friends know like 20 people. meta knows millions. that's the difference between staying small and scaling.

captions.label: C
captions.text: referrals = limited pipeline. ads = predictable pipeline.

lines.text: So you're waiting for referrals to come in. That's actually holding you back.
Referrals are based on how many people your customers know. That's a tiny pool. You're competing for scraps with everyone else hoping the phone rings.
The real problem is you have no control. You're at the mercy of word of mouth. One bad month, zero referrals. No strategy, no consistency.
With ads, you're reaching thousands of people actually searching for your service right now. You're not waiting. You're actively getting in front of people ready to hire.
Referrals plus ads means consistent leads and a business that doesn't depend on luck.

scripts.title: How a plumber cut his cost per lead to $38
scripts.overlay: text overlay with key stat or hook phrase
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, casual setup (kitchen or home office works), looking directly at camera. Relaxed but engaged. Let him gesture naturally when explaining the three changes.

captions.label: A
captions.text: his first mistake was targeting too wide. then everything changed.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most plumbers waste money on broad targeting. this guy narrowed his radius, tightened his keywords, and killed his cpl. here's what shifted.

captions.label: C
captions.text: three changes dropped his cost per lead from $120 to $38.

lines.text: This plumber was spending $120 for every lead. Then he made three changes and cut it down to $38. Let me show you what he actually did.
First thing — he stopped targeting the entire city. He narrowed it down to three neighborhoods where his average job value was highest. People in those areas booked faster and had bigger jobs.

Second, he tightened his ad copy to be super specific. Instead of 'plumbing services,' he was saying 'emergency leak repair under $500' or 'water heater replacement same day.' His click-through rate went up, his wasted clicks went down.
Third — and this one matters — he tested his landing page. Simple stuff. Phone number bigger, form simpler, and one clear call to action. More people actually contacted him from the same traffic.
The math is simple: smarter targeting, better messaging, cleaner funnel. That's how you drop your cost per lead without increasing your budget.

scripts.title: Why service businesses win with Meta ads
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, direct eye contact. He's in a home office or casual space. Natural lighting. No background clutter. He leans in slightly when making key points — feels like a genuine conversation, not a presentation.

captions.label: A
captions.text: service businesses have the easiest path to profitable ads on meta

captions.label: B
captions.text:
here's why plumbers, cleaners, and detailers outperform other niches on meta:

— local intent is built in
— high customer lifetime value
— short sales cycle
— recurring revenue

that's the advantage

captions.label: C
captions.text: local intent + high ltv + short sales cycle = profitable ads

lines.text: if you run a service business and you're not using Meta ads yet, you're actually sitting on the easiest path to consistent leads

the person searching for a plumber or a window cleaner is already looking to hire someone right now. they're not browsing, they're not comparing a hundred options. they need the work done. that intent is already there.

and because you're local, your customer lifetime value is insane compared to other businesses. one plumbing client calls you back for maintenance, for repairs, for emergencies. You're in their phone. That's recurring revenue.
a detailer gets paid in days. a landscaper books jobs in the same week. Your sales cycle is short. Meta shows results fast. You don't have to wait three months to know if ads are working.
That combination — local intent, high lifetime value, and a short sales cycle — is exactly what makes Meta profitable for service businesses. You've already got the hard part figured out.

scripts.title: Why ads actually do work for service businesses
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, direct to camera in natural light. Lean in slightly when delivering the hook. Maintain eye contact throughout. Let him use his hands naturally when explaining the three points.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service business owners tested ads wrong the first time

captions.label: B
captions.text: they think ads don't work because they set them up wrong. here's what actually fails: no clear offer, no local targeting, stopping after 2 weeks

captions.label: C
captions.text: ads work. you just gotta know what you're doing

lines.text: I talk to service business owners all the time who say ads don't work for them. And I get it — they tried it once, spent two hundred bucks, got nothing back, and decided it wasn't for them. But here's the thing: that's not because ads don't work. That's because something else went wrong.

Most of the time, it's the offer. You're running an ad with no clear reason for someone to call you right now. Like, a plumber just saying 'we do plumbing' isn't enough. But 'emergency plumbing, same-day service, no call-out fee' — that gets calls.

Second thing: targeting. You gotta be hitting the right area, the right distance from your business. If you're a window cleaner in Phoenix and your ads are showing up in Flagstaff, nothing's gonna happen. Local targeting isn't optional — it's everything.

And third — and this kills most people — they give up too fast. They'll run ads for a week, maybe two, see no results, and shut it down. But consistent $25 a day for a month? That's when you start seeing real leads come in.
Ads work. I've seen it happen a hundred times. You just gotta set them up right and actually let them run.
